**Jean Vendeville** (24 June 1527 – 15 October 1592) was a [law professor](/wiki/Law_professor "Law professor") and a [bishop of Tournai](/wiki/Bishop_of_Tournai "Bishop of Tournai").

## Life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jean_Vendeville&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Life")]

Vendeville was possibly born in [Lille](/wiki/Lille "Lille"), the son of Guillaume Vendeville and Marie Des Barbieux.[[1]](#cite_note-Possoz-1) He went to school in [Menin](/wiki/Menen "Menen"), and from the age of fifteen in Paris, where he studied law, beginning a legal practice in [Arras](/wiki/Arras "Arras"). In 1551 he married Anne Roelofs, of [Leuven](/wiki/Leuven "Leuven"), and in 1553 he obtained a doctorate in laws from the [University of Leuven](/wiki/Old_University_of_Leuven "Old University of Leuven").[[1]](#cite_note-Possoz-1) In 1562 he was appointed professor of law at the newly founded [University of Douai](/wiki/University_of_Douai "University of Douai"). He was influential in rallying secular support for the first establishment of [diocesan seminaries](/wiki/Seminary "Seminary") in the Low Countries, and for the establishment of a [Jesuit](/wiki/Jesuit "Jesuit") college at [Douai](/wiki/Douai "Douai"). He travelled to Rome to promote the establishment of missionary seminaries, and journeyed back to the Low Countries in company with [William Allen](/wiki/William_Allen_(cardinal) "William Allen (cardinal)"), whom he encouraged to found an [English College](/wiki/English_College,_Douai "English College, Douai") at Douai.[[1]](#cite_note-Possoz-1)

Vendeville was widowed in the early stages of the [Dutch Revolt](/wiki/Dutch_Revolt "Dutch Revolt"), and briefly went into exile as a public supporter of the royal cause.[[1]](#cite_note-Possoz-1) He conducted negotiations on behalf of the royalist interest in the Low Countries, and was named a privy councillor by [Philip II of Spain](/wiki/Philip_II_of_Spain "Philip II of Spain"), but in 1580 he resigned from public life to enter [holy orders](/wiki/Holy_orders "Holy orders"). He was ordained priest in 1581.[[1]](#cite_note-Possoz-1) He was particularly noted for his charity during the dearth of 1586. The king proposed him as bishop of [Tournai](/wiki/Tournai "Tournai") in 1586, the appointment was confirmed in 1587, and he was consecrated as bishop on 29 May 1588. In 1589 he called a synod for the implementation of the Tridentine reforms in his diocese.[[1]](#cite_note-Possoz-1)

He died on 15 October 1592 and was buried the next day in the choir of [Tournai Cathedral](/wiki/Tournai_Cathedral "Tournai Cathedral").[[1]](#cite_note-Possoz-1)
